splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity trumps agency deference (Auer/Kisor deference) when interpreting ambiguous provisions in the Sentencing Guidelines or criminal statutes.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether courts should apply the rule of lenity or defer to the Sentencing Commission's commentary when interpreting ambiguous criminal sentencing provisions. This matters because it determines whether ambiguity in criminal sentencing is resolved in favor of the defendant or in favor of the government's interpretation.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity takes precedence over agency deference when interpreting ambiguous criminal statutes or sentencing guidelines, resolving ambiguity in favor of the defendant.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that agency deference (Auer/Kisor deference to the Sentencing Commission's commentary) takes precedence over the rule of lenity when interpreting ambiguous Sentencing Guidelines provisions.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Sentencing Commission's Application Note 1 to U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2, which includes inchoate offenses (conspiracy, attempt, aiding and abetting) in the definition of 'controlled substance offense,' is entitled to deference when the guideline text does not mention inchoate offenses.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether Application Note 1's expansion of 'controlled substance offense' to include conspiracy and other inchoate offenses is a valid authoritative interpretation of § 4B1.2(b) under Stinson/Kisor. This affects whether defendants with prior conspiracy convictions qualify as career offenders under the Guidelines.

positions.label: Commentary is entitled to deference
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1 is a reasonable interpretation of a genuinely ambiguous guideline and is entitled to controlling weight, thus inchoate offenses qualify as controlled substance offenses.

positions.label: Commentary exceeds guideline text
positions.summary: Holds that the guideline text unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses, and Application Note 1 impermissibly expands the definition beyond what the text allows, so no deference is warranted.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify a defendant) or disjunctive (any single criterion disqualifies).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply split on whether a defendant must meet all three criminal-history criteria in § 3553(f)(1) to be disqualified from safety-valve relief, or whether meeting any one criterion is sufficient. This significantly impacts mandatory minimum sentences for drug offenses.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three required
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), reading 'and' in its natural conjunctive sense.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive or distributive — any one suffices
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria in § 3553(f)(1) is sufficient to disqualify a defendant from safety-valve relief.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Sentencing Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' in U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2(b) encompasses state drug convictions for substances not listed on the federal Controlled Substances Act schedules.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Guidelines when the state schedule includes substances not controlled under federal law. This affects career offender and other sentencing enhancements.

positions.label: State law controls — no federal limitation
positions.summary: Holds that § 4B1.2(b) broadly defines 'controlled substance offense' to include state law offenses even if the particular substance is not controlled under the federal Controlled Substances Act.

positions.label: Federal schedule required
positions.summary: Holds that a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' only if the substance is also controlled under the federal Controlled Substances Act.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether non-retroactive changes in sentencing law, such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c), can contribute to a finding of 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether courts may consider non-retroactive changes in law as part of the extraordinary-and-compelling-reasons analysis for compassionate release, or whether such changes are categorically excluded. This affects thousands of prisoners serving sentences that would be lower under current law.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may be considered
positions.summary: Holds that non-retroactive changes in sentencing law may be considered as one factor among others in determining whether extraordinary and compelling reasons warrant a sentence reduction.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes categorically excluded
positions.summary: Holds that a non-retroactive change in sentencing law cannot contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the civil or criminal appeal deadline applies to an order denying attorney's fees under the Hyde Amendment, which permits fee recovery in criminal cases where the prosecution was frivolous, vexatious, or in bad faith.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter (with a 30-day appeal deadline) or a criminal matter (with a 14-day deadline). The Seventh Circuit joined the majority view that the civil deadline applies because the fee motion is ancillary to the criminal case.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, so the 30-day civil appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(a) applies.

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment fee motion arises from a criminal case and thus the 14-day criminal appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(b) governs.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: United States v. Manchester Farming Partnership

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of a debtor's asset belong to the bankruptcy estate or to the debtor upon good-faith conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether appreciation in property value between the filing of a Chapter 13 petition and conversion to Chapter 7 inures to the bankruptcy estate or remains with the debtor. This significantly affects debtors who convert in good faith after property values increase.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that the plain language of § 348(f)(1)(A) and the broad scope of § 541(a) mean that post-petition appreciation inures to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion, not to the debtor.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that the Bankruptcy Code's structure, policies, and legislative history establish that post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation belongs to the debtor upon good-faith conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may expressly punish defendants for violations of supervised release when revoking supervision and imposing imprisonment, or whether revocation sentences must be based solely on the breach of trust and the need for sanction rather than retributive punishment.
splits.description: The Sixth Circuit allows district courts to punish defendants for supervised release violations, placing it at the extreme of a circuit split with circuits that hold revocation sentences should focus on breach of trust and deterrence rather than punishment for the underlying violation conduct.

positions.label: Courts may punish for violations
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may expressly punish defendants for violations of supervised release, allowing punishment as a permissible basis for revocation sentencing.

positions.label: Courts may not punish for violations
positions.summary: Holds that revocation sentences should be based on breach of trust and need for sanction, not retributive punishment for the violation conduct, consistent with the statutory text and Supreme Court precedent.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether an order denying a motion to seal court documents referencing private medical treatments and diagnoses is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine as 'effectively unreviewable' on appeal from final judgment.
splits.description: The D.C. Circuit held that medical privacy interests are of sufficiently 'high order' to warrant immediate appellate review of orders denying motions to seal medical information. This implicates a broader question about which privacy interests support collateral order jurisdiction, on which circuits may differ.

positions.label: Immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that maintaining privacy in medical treatments and diagnoses is a value of 'high order' warranting immediate appellate review under the collateral order doctrine because disclosure cannot be undone after final judgment.

positions.label: Not immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that orders denying motions to seal or protect documents, including those with medical information, are not collateral orders because alternative remedies exist, such as protective orders or mandamus.
